evidence.text:
Duration (months):
18

Detailed Research Plan:
Detailed research plan (5000 words) – currently 5034
What is the problem being addressed?
Dizziness
Dizziness ranks among the top reasons for visiting a doctor, and affects up to 35% of individuals
at some point in their lives. It can be due to neurological, inner ear (vestibular), cardiovascular,
psychological or other disorders.
A German study provides the most cited population wide estimate of the incidence and
prevalence of vertigo caused by vestibular conditions (Neuhauser et al 2005). They reported
vertigo was caused by vestibular conditions in 24% of cases, had a lifetime prevalence of 7.4%
between the ages of 18-79 years old, and a 1-year incidence of 1.4%.
What is the vestibular system?
The vestibular system incorporates the inner ear (vestibular apparatus), vestibular nuclei in the
brainstem, the eighth cranial nerve and the cerebellum. It is responsible for maintaining clarity
of vision and contributing to our control of upright balance and movement.
The vestibular apparatus is located in the temporal bone on both sides of the head. It consists
of a bony and membranous labyrinth, in turn filled with perilymph and endolymph fluid. The
membranous labyrinth is formed of three semicircular canals and two otolith areas. They
detect angular and linear accelerations respectively, as well as the otoliths also detecting head
position. The receptor areas are characterised by hair cells covered by a gelatinous membrane,
with each hair cell connected to the eighth cranial nerve. The receptor areas differ in that the
otoliths have calcium carbonate crystals embedded on the receptor gelatinous membrane.
Damage to any part of the vestibular system can result in dizziness, vertigo, blurred vision,
imbalance and falls. Symptoms can vary and may be spontaneous, triggered by movement or
environments, the result of exposure or trauma and can also be persistent.
Positional dizziness
Positional dizziness is triggered by changes in head position relative to gravity's influence. For
example, getting into bed, rolling in bed and bending over. It can result in dizziness, vertigo, and
blurred vision, with unsteadiness commonly reported. The symptoms are typically brief, but
can have a significant impact on a persons independence in everyday activities (DHI Ref no 4
Dan), mood, walking, confidence and can cause falls.
Positional dizziness can be due to inner ear or brain disorders. Gold standard positioning
manaourvres should be conducted to help make a diagnosis (Ref). However, these tests rely on
correct interpretation of the induced eye movement (nystagmus) and this can be challenging
especially for someone new to assessing dizziness (Male et al …; Meldrum et al …).
It is important to identify the underlying disorder, as the treatment and management will be very
different. Repositioning manaourvres successfully treat and resolve BPPV, whereas in central
disorders, these do not work and medication is typically required alongside treatment to
address any underlying structural cause.

Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the
public and/or to patients/service users, carers, and health and care services?
This research has been developed following patient and service user feedback regarding the
challenges in assessing, identifying and treatment positional dizziness.
A 35 year old black female recently reported "…I am frustrated that I have had to suffer with this
horrible dizziness for so long. Why couldn't I see someone to sort it out quicker as this has
stopped me living my life?...".
Additionally, a 66 year old white female reported "…everyone kept asking me if I was dizzy, and I
kept telling them no. But I was really unsteady getting out of bed, and when I looked up. My
doctor referred me for physio thinking it was my balance due to my hip and back pain. It wasn't
until you did the tests that you found the real cause was in my ears and cured me in a matter of
minutes…".
These two quotes highlight some of the common complaints voiced by patients I have worked
with over my clinical career. Accessing effective assessment and treatment of a peripheral
condition, such as BPPV, can reduce the risk of falls and injuries (Ref), allow someone to
independently live their lives, and reduce the burden on the healthcare and social care services.
In the elderly, falls can lead to death, wither as a result of the fall or indirectly (Ref 5 Dan).
Whilst no cost analysis has been presented in the research, enabling people to remain active is
crucial for general health and wellbeing and is advocated by both the NHS (Ref) and World
Health Organisation Physical Activity Guidelines (Ref).
Another patient that raised concerns and a desire for change was a doctor that came to see me.
She was a 32 year old female who had been self-treating her positional dizziness for several
weeks, but was vomiting afterwards and the issue was not resolving. Whilst her nystagmus
appeared to consistent with a peripheral cause, the lack of resolution and vomiting concerned
me (MacDonald…). In collaboration with a medical colleague, and after a through examination
to include a clear brain scan, a central positional diagnosis was made. Specifically, vestibular
migraine. My colleague started her on migraine prophylaxis and her dizziness completely
resolved. She reported "…I did not know that I had migraine as I do not have a headache, and
thought only BPPV caused this type of dizziness…A better awareness of this problem is
needed". The stereotype that migraine means bad headache, unfortunately means it frequently
goes un-diagnosed.
I have been involved in training GPs and other medical professionals, in the assessment of
dizziness, and more specifically in recognising BPPV. At these events, GP's have told me "…they
do not have time…", "…prefer to signpost patients to someone else to manage the problem…",
and "…do not feel confident in interpreting the eye movements…". This latter point was also
seen in a recent acute vertigo study, whereby doctors in the accident and emergency
department made diagnoses of BPPV without doing the required tests, and diagnosed BPPV
when a proportion of them actually had a central diagnosis instead (Ref).
This research aims to simplify the diagnosis of peripheral versus central causes of positional
dizziness and unsteadiness, and thus reduce the time patients may be waiting to receive the
correct treatment, whether that be for a peripheral of central cause of positional dizziness.

Review of the existing evidence - how does the existing literature support this proposal?
Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo
The most common inner ear disorder, worldwide, is benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
(BPPV). In the previously mentioned German population interview (Ref), vertigo was due to
BPPV in 8%, had a lifetime prevalence of 2.4%, and a 1-year incidence of 0.6%. Furthermore,
they describe that 1 in 3 of participants who reported vertigo due to a vestibular disorder, were
diagnosed with BPPV.
Relying on the history taking alone, may fail to identify this treatable cause of dizziness or
unsteadiness. This was demonstrated in adults over the age of 60 years old, who were
diagnosed with BPPV, but did not report vertigo or dizziness, and instead conveyed
unsteadiness, imbalance and light-headedness (Li et al 2024; Batuecas-Caletrio et al 2013).
Additionally, vestibular agnosia has been identified in older adults, those with
neurodegeneration and following traumatic brain injury (Smith et al …). Here patients had a
positive positional test suggesting BPPV, that resolved with repositioning manaourvres, but the
patients did not perceive any illusion of movement resulting from the observed nystagmus.
These studies emphasise why positional tests should be completed in order to make a
diagnosis. Furthermore, as BPPV can cause falls, or be the result of a fall, the World Falls
Guidelines (Ref) have also strongly recommended that patients who have fallen, should have a
multifactorial falls risk assessment completed. They specify this includes a vestibular
assessment and positional tests, in order to conduct and manage any modifiable risk factors.
Diagnostic criteria have been published by the Barany Society (Von Brevern et al 2015), that
state the clinical features essential during canal specific positioning manoeuvres, to include
nystagmus latency, direction, and duration.
Each of the three canals, in either inner ear, can be affected by BPPV to different extents. The
most commonly affected is the posterior canal accounting for 85-95% of cases (Parnes et al
2003) and horizontal canal for 5-15% (Cakir et al 2006; White et al 2005). Anterior canal
involvement is reported as only 1%, with a central cause strongly suspected instead of a
peripheral cause (Korres et al 2002).
There are certain key treatment manaourvres, that are used to treat each canal that can be
affected by BPPV, for example the Epley manoeuvre. These treatment manaourvres move any
displaced otoconial debris out of the semicircular canals, by moving the patients head and
body, through a series of specific positions. Each specific position is designed to put the
affected canal under the maximal influence of gravity.
Resolution rates for the most common type of BPPV, after one treatment are 72-74% (Ref), and
increase to 90% after two treatments (Ref), depending on the manoeuvre selected. However,
there is limited evidence on the resolution rates of all the common sub types of BPPV, and there
is also lack of consensus on the number of treatments required to successfully resolve all types
of BPPV. This was highlighted in a survey I completed in 2024 to a group of 24 multidisciplinary
professionals, attending a specialist conference for dizziness in the UK.
BPPV can recur at a rate of xxx per year (Ref). Certain cardiovascular and metabolic risk factors
have been proposed, however there are differing outcomes in the literature and methodological
concerns in how these studies have been undertaken. Of interest, no studies to date, have

explored if there is an anatomical variation in the inner ear, specifically the canal orientation,
that may explain why the specific movements of the treatment procedures do not work. This is
one of the aims of this proposed study.
Central positional nystagmus
Brain disorders that can cause positional symptoms include damage to the cerebellum or
brainstem from stroke, tumours or more commonly due to non-structural causes such as
vestibular migraine (Lemos…).
Whilst specific nystagmus features can help identify positional dizziness from the brain, at
present the diagnosis is often one made due to failure of an inner ear cause (BPPV) to resolve
(Ref). Thus patients may need to attend multiple appointments in the health service prior to a
central cause being considered. Or, they may continue to have treatments, and a central cause
is never suspected.
However, there are gaps in the literature and a lack of consensus in how many treatments are
deeded adequate in order to resolve BPPV. Therefore relying on "failure to respond" as a
diagnostic feature, when there lacks certainty how many treatments should resolve BPPV, may
contribute to a lack of identification of central causes.
Central positional nystagmus (CPN) is also suspected when atypical forms of nystagmus are
seen. Yet, emerging research now suggests there may be atypical forms of BPPV that may
account for these nystagmus patterns, complicating the process of diagnosis, and increasing
the risk that brain causes may be overlooked and not managed.
Whilst the treatment manoeuvres for BPPV are safe (refs), the treatment manaourvres will not
resolve a central cause of positional nystagmus and dizziness. Furthermore, the resulting delay
in identifying that the cause is central, may lead to increased levels of anxiety, low mood, social
withdrawal, increased dependence on others and potentially falls.
When a central cause is identified, it is important to address any structural abnormality found
on a brain scan. This may involve certain medications or surgical intervention. However, the
most common central cause is vestibular migraine, and would not show any abnormality on a
brain scan. In this instance, migraine prophylaxis is recommended (Ref 35 Lemos). This was
the course of action taken by Mahrous and colleagues (Ref). They treated ten patients from a
case group of 12 with a specific medication used in migraine, and the positional symptoms
completely resolved. However, they only did this after treating them with BPPV manoeuvres
three times, assuming the positional symptoms had to be due to BPPV first.
Whilst CPN is recognised as a cause of positional nystagmus, there is a lack of awareness in
making the diagnosis both within the research literature and thus clinically. In a systematic
review of all the literature to date, Lemos and colleagues (Ref) recommend further research
using video goggles, whilst conducting standardised assessments using the testing manoeuvres
commonly used in BPPV. They also recommend longitudinal studies to evaluate patients over
time, and to understand the pathology in greater detail.
What are the research question/aims and objectives?
This study aims to prospectively assess patients presenting with dizziness and/or unsteadiness
that is triggered by head and body position changes, in order to:

Primary aim
 characterise the nystagmus features and establish accompanying symptoms for
peripheral and central causes positional causes
Secondary aims
 determine the prevalence of peripheral and central causes of positional nystagmus in
the North Central London area
 identify the number of treatments required for each canal type of BPPV within the cohort
and through an International modified Delphi Consensus
 establish the recurrence rates for different types of BPPV
 explore if there is an anatomical difference to the inner ear for those with non resolving
BPPV
This study aims to expand the limited literature to help clinicians identify and distinguish
between peripheral and central positional nystagmus more effectively. This will improve access
to the correct treatment in a more timely manner, and enhance patient care and experience.
The study will also fill a knowledge gap in understanding how many treatments are required for
each sub type of BPPV. Prior to this application, I conducted a preliminary survey to explore the
overall management of BPPV across a group of 24 multidisciplinary professionals, attending a
specialist conference for dizziness in the UK. The results of this have informed the research
protocol, but a larger scale Delphi consensus opinion will be sought as part of this proposal to
inform best practice of BPPV, worldwide.
Finally, this study will include a novel case control exploration as to whether there is an
anatomical explanation for why BPPV does not respond to the gold standard manaourvres. This
will be the first study, to the applicant's knowledge, using 3D reconstructive magnetic
resonance imaging in peripheral positional nystagmus.
Project Plan
Observational study
Recruitment
Adults (over 16 years old) reporting dizziness, vertigo, or unsteadiness that is triggered by head
or body movement will be eligible to participate. Exclusion criteria include: medical instability
(cardiovascular, cervical, or neurological), spontaneous nystagmus in sitting, pregnancy,
previous history of existing and persistent vertigo, and lacking capacity to consent. Those
unable to understand English will also be excluded, as the questionnaires used in this study and
in the vestibular specialism, have not been validated and translated into every language.
This prospective study will be open for recruitment for 18 months. A purposive sample will be
recruited. Figure 1 summarises the flow of each participant through the study duration.

Figure 1: Participant flow through the study.

Power Calculations
Current research lacks detailed prevalence data on positional nystagmus across healthcare
settings, failing to differentiate between peripheral and central causes. The most relevant data
from a French neuro-otology clinic over nine months indicates that 20% of patients with
symptoms of vertigo and unsteadiness showed positional nystagmus, with breakdowns into
BPPV (80%), other peripheral causes (3%), central causes (12%), and undetermined (5%)
(Bertholon et al., 2006).
To ensure sufficient statistical power for detecting the 20% prevalence rate of positional
nystagmus identified in previous studies (Bertholon et al., 2006), we calculated the necessary
sample size using the formula n= [(z)2 (p)(1-p)]/(d)2 where Z is 1.96 for 95% confidence, p is 0.20,
and d is 0.05 (Sharma et al., 2019; Pourhoseingholi et al., 2013). This calculation resulted in a
sample size of approximately 246 adults, which is sufficient to provide reliable data while
ensuring the study remains scalable and manageable within the constraints of our resources.
Participant recruitment
The study will be conducted at a single site, based at The National Hospital for Neurology and
Neurosurgery. This will ensure access to specialist equipment (Balcare chair) for
standardisation of testing, to address the study aims and as recommended by researchers
(Lemos). The study will be embedded within the Acute vertigo service, and Same Day
Emergency Care service (SDEC), at University College London Hospitals (UCLH) with oversight
by Consultant Neurologists. This would also be the location patients from the North Central
London area would attend for routine clinical care, and hence this reduces any additional travel
burden or cost.
The North Central London (NCL) area is home to a diverse range of ethnicities, that includes
white, Asian, and Black people. The largest ethnic minority communities in this area of London
are Turkish, Irish, Polish and Asian. Discussions with patients from the NCL area, revealed they
wanted to be involved in this study, but raised concerns about covering the travel costs. One
person (Mr M) also raised concerns about other people he knew being able to fully understand
and speak English well enough to take part. Therefore, funding will be requested to cover travel
expenses and also cover costs of any interpreters that are required.
The study will be advertised through local GP network and community services for North
Central London. This will include approximately 200 GP surgeries, and community neurology
teams in Barnet, Camden, Enfield, Haringey, and Islington. It will also be advertised internally at
UCLH through email communication and attendance at departmental meetings. This broad
recruitment strategy will ensure the desired sample size will be met in the allocated recruitment
time.
Advertisements will include screening questions for positional dizziness and unsteadiness.
These questions have been adopted from a diagnostic framework for vestibular causes of
dizziness (Male et al), which had PPI involvement and a modified Delphi consensus.
If potential participants answer the questions as indicated on the advertisement, the participant
or clinician will be encouraged to contact the research team to make a referral. Patients helped
to determine the best recruitment approach, and reported they would like an email and a
telephone option. These contact details of the research team will be included on the
advertisements.

Participants will be able to self-refer, or be referred by their GP or community therapy services
from within North Central London areas. Internal referrals from UCLH emergency department
(ED), falls service, the hyper-acute stroke unit (HASU), and from therapy services will also be
accepted.
Potential participants will be contacted by a member of the research team and provided with
the participant information sheet by email. They will be given the opportunity to ask questions,
prior to providing consent. Consent will be provided electronically through a central REDCap
database set up for this project. If potential participants request the consent form in paper
format, the signed hard copy will be loaded in to the secure database.
They will then be invited to attend the hospital for assessment and treatment as the earliest
point in time. The GP will be informed of their participation in the study.
Data Management
Potential participants' data will be identifiable for the purpose of follow up, and potential 3D
scanning. However, it will be anonymised for any dissemination. All data will be kept
confidential and secure by the research team and stored electronically in a central REDCap
database. The REDCap database will require a password to access which will only be known by
the research team.
Eye movement recordings will be stored on the Balcare laptop and secure drive. This will
require a password to access, and the data will be handled securely in line with UCLH routine
data governance regulations.
Research protocol
Participants will electronically complete routine questionnaires used in dizziness and balance
services (see Table 1) and provide more personal information relating to their health. This will
help identify any accompanying symptoms for peripheral and central causes of positional
nystagmus.
Patient reported questionnaire Details
Dizziness handicap inventory (DHI) A measure of self-perceived disability
attributable to vestibular disease.
Subjects score 25 questions which are
classified into three domain levels; Yes = 4,
Sometimes = 2, No = 0 with a total
percentage score calculated.
Scores >20 indicate dizziness/unsteadiness
is having a significant impact on their life
(Jacobson and Newman, 1990).
Situational Vertigo Questionnaire (SVQ) Evaluates the extent that certain situations
cause symptoms of vertigo or makes patients
feel worse.
Each activity rated between 0 (not at all) and
4 (very much) with a not tried (NT) option
available.
Scores >1 indicate visual induced dizziness
and/or imbalance (Guerraz et al., 2001).
State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI – form Y) A self-reported assessment of trait and state
anxiety.

Subjects rate 20 statements using a 4-point
Likert scale between 1 (not at all) to 4 (very
much so).
Higher scores indicate greater anxiety (Julian,
2011).
Visually Induced Motion Sickness A self reported tool to estimate the
Susceptibility Questionnaire (VIMSSQ - short) susceptibility to motion sickness when using
devices and watching moving visual content.
Subjects rate six devices for how often they
experience nausea, dizziness, headache,
fatigue, and eye strain using a 4-point Likert
scale.
Higher scores indicate greater motion
sickness susceptibility (Golding et al 2021)
Table 1: Details of the questionnaires each participant will complete as part of the initial visit.
Participants will then be assessed, by a specialist physiotherapist, using a purpose-built chair
(Balcare). This chair allows accurate assessment, irrespective of the patient's physical mobility
level or co-morbidities, and without any increased manual handling demands for the clinician. It
provides a standardised approach to assessment and uses video goggles that helps clinicians
to interpret different types of nystagmus. This will allow more accurate interpretation and
quantification of peripheral positional nystagmus from central positional nystagmus.
Each assessment visit will take no more than one hour. It will include a lying and standing blood
pressure check for any postural hypotension, and a full eye movement examination in sitting
using the video goggles (fixation removed). The eye movement examination includes observing
for spontaneous nystagmus, gaze evoked nystagmus, and abnormalities in smooth pursuit and
saccades. Positional tests using the Balcare chair will then be completed, whilst wearing the
video goggles. These will include Dix Hallpike on both sides, Roll Tests, straight head hanging
and sitting back up.
Should BPPV be identified as per the diagnostic criteria (ref), then the appropriate treatment
manoeuvre will be provided once using the Balcare chair. This may include the modified Epley
manoeuvre, Semont liberatory manoeuvre, Gufoni manoeuvre (geotropic or apogeotropic),
Lempert BBQ roll, or the Yacovino manoeuvre (Refs?). Each of these manoeuvres take no more
than 3 minutes to complete using the Balcare chair.
These patients will be followed up at the next convenient time (within four weeks) and re-treated
once per visit if required, in order to make sure the problem has fully resolved. They will be
invited to re-attend for subsequent re-assessment and treatment, at four weekly intervals up to
a maximum of three treatments, as informed by the preparatory survey of specialists. In this
time, if the assessment manoeuvres are negative, this will end the participants involvement in
the study.
Any patients that do not respond to three treatments, will then be referred to the acute vertigo
service for review by a Consultant Neurologist, as per the consensus of specialists from the
preparatory survey. They will also be invited to have high resolution 3D reconstructive magnetic
resonance imaging of the inner ear. The imaging will be compared to a purposive group of BPPV
patients (n=12) whose symptoms resolved within three manoeuvres to detect any anatomical
difference that may explain the lack of resolution. Differences in the angle, orientation, width,
and the viscosity of the endolymph will be compared to responders, using computational

methods. This may then allow adjustments to be made to the treatment sequence to optimally
treat and resolve the BPPV.
Participants that do not have the nystagmus characteristics expected for BPPV within the
positional tests, or present with abnormalities in the oculomotor examination indicating central
problems, will be referred to the acute vertigo service for further assessment and management
by a Neurologist. This may include medication or further imaging. This will end the participants
involvement in the study.
Any participant will be invited to contact the research team, at any point during the recruitment
phase of the study, should they feel their symptoms have recurred. They will then follow the
same intervention pathway as new participants (see Figure 1).
Statistical analysis
Descriptive and predictive statistical analyses will be performed. Categorical data will be
presented as frequencies with percentages. Continuous data will be presented as means and
Standard Deviations (±SD) where data is normally distributed. Medians and Inter-Quartile
Ranges (IQR) will be used when the data is not normally distributed. Appropriate regression
models will be used to investigate the association between individual clinical features and
diagnoses of peripheral or central causes of positional nystagmus/dizziness.
3D reconstructive MRI scans of participants not responding to BPPV manoeuvres, will be
compared to those that do respond to explore if there is an anatomical difference. How?
Delphi expert opinion
In addition to this observational study, a modified Delphi technique will be used to gain
consensus from a multi disciplinary international group of clinicians working with vestibular
conditions (Trevelyan and Robinson, 2015). This will include Consultants with a primary interest
and with >5 years experience in clinical neuro-otology or audiovestibular medicine, ENT
Consultants, Neurology Consultants or GPs working with dizziness. In addition, physical
therapists, and audiologists with >5 year's experience of working with vestibular conditions will
also be included. There will be three rounds to the Delphi process.
Participants will be identified through the membership lists of the European Academy of
Neurology (EAN) specialist panel of neuro-otologists, Barany Society, the vestibular
rehabilitation special interest groups for the American Physical Therapy Association (APTA), the
Association of Chartered Physiotherapists Interested in Vestibular Rehabiliation (ACPIVR), and
the British Society of Audiology (BSA).
Round one
This will be a survey to explore participants management of the different subtypes of BPPV,
expanding the preparatory survey results. The responses will be anonymous and confidential.
Membership secretaries will be sent an email by a member of the research team, with specific
wording to forward on to members asking them to participate. Within the email invitation, will be
a link for the free survey using Google forms.
The membership secretaries will send a reminder email one week later, in order to maximise
participation.

Round two
Round two will be informed by round one. Statements will be developed from the survey
answers with the highest scores, and these will be emailed via the membership secretaries of
the above mentioned international groups.
Respondents will be asked to rate how much they agree or disagree with the statements using a
six-point scale. Choosing a scale without a midpoint will avoid neutral responses and will make
respondents make a positive or negative decision about each question (Trevelyan and
Robinson, 2015). They will also be asked to add any statements they feel should be included or
suggest any that need rewording.
The membership secretaries will send a reminder email one week later. Consensus per
statement will be defined when >75% of the responses are in the lower or higher tertile
(Boulkedid et al., 2011).
The responses will be anonymous and confidential. However, at this stage, respondents will be
asked if they would like to be part of a panel discussion in order to discuss any points that have
not reached consensus. If respondents are interested, they will be asked to provide an email
address and name so they can be contacted.
Round three
This will involve a panel discussion of volunteers from round two. A time will be established that
will accommodate as many of those interested as possible, accounting for time differences and
the individuals' commitments.
The panel discussion will attempt to reach a majority consensus on any remaining statements.
Any points that do not reach majority consensus, will have recommendations made for future
research.
Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
The results from this project will be submitted to relevant peer reviewed journals for publication.
I anticipate at least three papers that will include describing the nystagmus features of the
largest cohort of patients with positional nystagmus and any key distinguishing features, sharing
the outcome of the International and multidisciplinary Delphi consensus, and a paper to share
the findings from the first study to date undertaking 3D reconstructive imaging of the inner ear.
I will also submit these results to specialist conferences, to present as either a poster or
platform presentation. This includes the Barany Society, and the British Society of Neuro-
Otology. These conferences are attended by specialist doctors, physiotherapists, audiologists,
and other members of the multidisciplinary team working with dizziness and balance patients,
and are internationally renowned. ? ED/other medical ones ?
As a member of the physiotherapy interest group (ACPIVR), I will also share my results at either
annual national or regional training events, and through the quarterly journal. I will embed the
findings in any education I continue to provide through national and international courses, and
through university masters programs.
I will also share the results through a range of social media platforms, including the research
unit at UCL, ACPIVR, my own personal accounts, and the Ménière's Disease society. I will also

share the findings as a newsletter to the service users that assisted in recruitment, both in the
community and at UCLH. I will also offer to present the findings at any local departmental
meetings.
As part of this project, I plan to set up a PPI advisory group and will seek their help on how best
to disseminate the findings so results are shared with the people it affects, and improves care in
the future.
Project management
The project has been discussed with the NIHR Research Support Service (RSS) and xxxxx at the
UCL/UCLH Joint Research Office. The project has been developed with support from a
specialist Neurologist that leads the SENSE research unit in the Institute of Neurology and a
specialist Neuroradiologist.
Costings have been approved by the xxxxx based in xxxxx.
Ethics
Once funding is approved, I will apply for internal ethical approval through the Joint Research
Office at UCLH. I will also complete the Integrated Research Application System (IRAS) form
and apply for NHS ethical approval, as this study will include patients.
Success criteria

evidence.text:
Duration (months):
18

Detailed Research Plan:
Detailed research plan (5000 words) – currently 5034
What is the problem being addressed?
Dizziness
Dizziness ranks among the top reasons for visiting a doctor, and affects up to 35% of individuals
at some point in their lives. It can be due to neurological, inner ear (vestibular), cardiovascular,
psychological or other disorders.
A German study provides the most cited population wide estimate of the incidence and
prevalence of vertigo caused by vestibular conditions (Neuhauser et al 2005). They reported
vertigo was caused by vestibular conditions in 24% of cases, had a lifetime prevalence of 7.4%
between the ages of 18-79 years old, and a 1-year incidence of 1.4%.
What is the vestibular system?
The vestibular system incorporates the inner ear (vestibular apparatus), vestibular nuclei in the
brainstem, the eighth cranial nerve and the cerebellum. It is responsible for maintaining clarity
of vision and contributing to our control of upright balance and movement.
The vestibular apparatus is located in the temporal bone on both sides of the head. It consists
of a bony and membranous labyrinth, in turn filled with perilymph and endolymph fluid. The
membranous labyrinth is formed of three semicircular canals and two otolith areas. They
detect angular and linear accelerations respectively, as well as the otoliths also detecting head
position. The receptor areas are characterised by hair cells covered by a gelatinous membrane,
with each hair cell connected to the eighth cranial nerve. The receptor areas differ in that the
otoliths have calcium carbonate crystals embedded on the receptor gelatinous membrane.
Damage to any part of the vestibular system can result in dizziness, vertigo, blurred vision,
imbalance and falls. Symptoms can vary and may be spontaneous, triggered by movement or
environments, the result of exposure or trauma and can also be persistent.
Positional dizziness
Positional dizziness is triggered by changes in head position relative to gravity's influence. For
example, getting into bed, rolling in bed and bending over. It can result in dizziness, vertigo, and
blurred vision, with unsteadiness commonly reported. The symptoms are typically brief, but
can have a significant impact on a persons independence in everyday activities (DHI Ref no 4
Dan), mood, walking, confidence and can cause falls.
Positional dizziness can be due to inner ear or brain disorders. Gold standard positioning
manaourvres should be conducted to help make a diagnosis (Ref). However, these tests rely on
correct interpretation of the induced eye movement (nystagmus) and this can be challenging
especially for someone new to assessing dizziness (Male et al …; Meldrum et al …).
It is important to identify the underlying disorder, as the treatment and management will be very
different. Repositioning manaourvres successfully treat and resolve BPPV, whereas in central
disorders, these do not work and medication is typically required alongside treatment to
address any underlying structural cause.

Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the
public and/or to patients/service users, carers, and health and care services?
This research has been developed following patient and service user feedback regarding the
challenges in assessing, identifying and treatment positional dizziness.
A 35 year old black female recently reported "…I am frustrated that I have had to suffer with this
horrible dizziness for so long. Why couldn't I see someone to sort it out quicker as this has
stopped me living my life?...".
Additionally, a 66 year old white female reported "…everyone kept asking me if I was dizzy, and I
kept telling them no. But I was really unsteady getting out of bed, and when I looked up. My
doctor referred me for physio thinking it was my balance due to my hip and back pain. It wasn't
until you did the tests that you found the real cause was in my ears and cured me in a matter of
minutes…".
These two quotes highlight some of the common complaints voiced by patients I have worked
with over my clinical career. Accessing effective assessment and treatment of a peripheral
condition, such as BPPV, can reduce the risk of falls and injuries (Ref), allow someone to
independently live their lives, and reduce the burden on the healthcare and social care services.
In the elderly, falls can lead to death, wither as a result of the fall or indirectly (Ref 5 Dan).
Whilst no cost analysis has been presented in the research, enabling people to remain active is
crucial for general health and wellbeing and is advocated by both the NHS (Ref) and World
Health Organisation Physical Activity Guidelines (Ref).
Another patient that raised concerns and a desire for change was a doctor that came to see me.
She was a 32 year old female who had been self-treating her positional dizziness for several
weeks, but was vomiting afterwards and the issue was not resolving. Whilst her nystagmus
appeared to consistent with a peripheral cause, the lack of resolution and vomiting concerned
me (MacDonald…). In collaboration with a medical colleague, and after a through examination
to include a clear brain scan, a central positional diagnosis was made. Specifically, vestibular
migraine. My colleague started her on migraine prophylaxis and her dizziness completely
resolved. She reported "…I did not know that I had migraine as I do not have a headache, and
thought only BPPV caused this type of dizziness…A better awareness of this problem is
needed". The stereotype that migraine means bad headache, unfortunately means it frequently
goes un-diagnosed.
I have been involved in training GPs and other medical professionals, in the assessment of
dizziness, and more specifically in recognising BPPV. At these events, GP's have told me "…they
do not have time…", "…prefer to signpost patients to someone else to manage the problem…",
and "…do not feel confident in interpreting the eye movements…". This latter point was also
seen in a recent acute vertigo study, whereby doctors in the accident and emergency
department made diagnoses of BPPV without doing the required tests, and diagnosed BPPV
when a proportion of them actually had a central diagnosis instead (Ref).
This research aims to simplify the diagnosis of peripheral versus central causes of positional
dizziness and unsteadiness, and thus reduce the time patients may be waiting to receive the
correct treatment, whether that be for a peripheral of central cause of positional dizziness.

Review of the existing evidence - how does the existing literature support this proposal?
Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo
The most common inner ear disorder, worldwide, is benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
(BPPV). In the previously mentioned German population interview (Ref), vertigo was due to
BPPV in 8%, had a lifetime prevalence of 2.4%, and a 1-year incidence of 0.6%. Furthermore,
they describe that 1 in 3 of participants who reported vertigo due to a vestibular disorder, were
diagnosed with BPPV.
Relying on the history taking alone, may fail to identify this treatable cause of dizziness or
unsteadiness. This was demonstrated in adults over the age of 60 years old, who were
diagnosed with BPPV, but did not report vertigo or dizziness, and instead conveyed
unsteadiness, imbalance and light-headedness (Li et al 2024; Batuecas-Caletrio et al 2013).
Additionally, vestibular agnosia has been identified in older adults, those with
neurodegeneration and following traumatic brain injury (Smith et al …). Here patients had a
positive positional test suggesting BPPV, that resolved with repositioning manaourvres, but the
patients did not perceive any illusion of movement resulting from the observed nystagmus.
These studies emphasise why positional tests should be completed in order to make a
diagnosis. Furthermore, as BPPV can cause falls, or be the result of a fall, the World Falls
Guidelines (Ref) have also strongly recommended that patients who have fallen, should have a
multifactorial falls risk assessment completed. They specify this includes a vestibular
assessment and positional tests, in order to conduct and manage any modifiable risk factors.
Diagnostic criteria have been published by the Barany Society (Von Brevern et al 2015), that
state the clinical features essential during canal specific positioning manoeuvres, to include
nystagmus latency, direction, and duration.
Each of the three canals, in either inner ear, can be affected by BPPV to different extents. The
most commonly affected is the posterior canal accounting for 85-95% of cases (Parnes et al
2003) and horizontal canal for 5-15% (Cakir et al 2006; White et al 2005). Anterior canal
involvement is reported as only 1%, with a central cause strongly suspected instead of a
peripheral cause (Korres et al 2002).
There are certain key treatment manaourvres, that are used to treat each canal that can be
affected by BPPV, for example the Epley manoeuvre. These treatment manaourvres move any
displaced otoconial debris out of the semicircular canals, by moving the patients head and
body, through a series of specific positions. Each specific position is designed to put the
affected canal under the maximal influence of gravity.
Resolution rates for the most common type of BPPV, after one treatment are 72-74% (Ref), and
increase to 90% after two treatments (Ref), depending on the manoeuvre selected. However,
there is limited evidence on the resolution rates of all the common sub types of BPPV, and there
is also lack of consensus on the number of treatments required to successfully resolve all types
of BPPV. This was highlighted in a survey I completed in 2024 to a group of 24 multidisciplinary
professionals, attending a specialist conference for dizziness in the UK.
BPPV can recur at a rate of xxx per year (Ref). Certain cardiovascular and metabolic risk factors
have been proposed, however there are differing outcomes in the literature and methodological
concerns in how these studies have been undertaken. Of interest, no studies to date, have

explored if there is an anatomical variation in the inner ear, specifically the canal orientation,
that may explain why the specific movements of the treatment procedures do not work. This is
one of the aims of this proposed study.
Central positional nystagmus
Brain disorders that can cause positional symptoms include damage to the cerebellum or
brainstem from stroke, tumours or more commonly due to non-structural causes such as
vestibular migraine (Lemos…).
Whilst specific nystagmus features can help identify positional dizziness from the brain, at
present the diagnosis is often one made due to failure of an inner ear cause (BPPV) to resolve
(Ref). Thus patients may need to attend multiple appointments in the health service prior to a
central cause being considered. Or, they may continue to have treatments, and a central cause
is never suspected.
However, there are gaps in the literature and a lack of consensus in how many treatments are
deeded adequate in order to resolve BPPV. Therefore relying on "failure to respond" as a
diagnostic feature, when there lacks certainty how many treatments should resolve BPPV, may
contribute to a lack of identification of central causes.
Central positional nystagmus (CPN) is also suspected when atypical forms of nystagmus are
seen. Yet, emerging research now suggests there may be atypical forms of BPPV that may
account for these nystagmus patterns, complicating the process of diagnosis, and increasing
the risk that brain causes may be overlooked and not managed.
Whilst the treatment manoeuvres for BPPV are safe (refs), the treatment manaourvres will not
resolve a central cause of positional nystagmus and dizziness. Furthermore, the resulting delay
in identifying that the cause is central, may lead to increased levels of anxiety, low mood, social
withdrawal, increased dependence on others and potentially falls.
When a central cause is identified, it is important to address any structural abnormality found
on a brain scan. This may involve certain medications or surgical intervention. However, the
most common central cause is vestibular migraine, and would not show any abnormality on a
brain scan. In this instance, migraine prophylaxis is recommended (Ref 35 Lemos). This was
the course of action taken by Mahrous and colleagues (Ref). They treated ten patients from a
case group of 12 with a specific medication used in migraine, and the positional symptoms
completely resolved. However, they only did this after treating them with BPPV manoeuvres
three times, assuming the positional symptoms had to be due to BPPV first.
Whilst CPN is recognised as a cause of positional nystagmus, there is a lack of awareness in
making the diagnosis both within the research literature and thus clinically. In a systematic
review of all the literature to date, Lemos and colleagues (Ref) recommend further research
using video goggles, whilst conducting standardised assessments using the testing manoeuvres
commonly used in BPPV. They also recommend longitudinal studies to evaluate patients over
time, and to understand the pathology in greater detail.
What are the research question/aims and objectives?
This study aims to prospectively assess patients presenting with dizziness and/or unsteadiness
that is triggered by head and body position changes, in order to:

Primary aim
 characterise the nystagmus features and establish accompanying symptoms for
peripheral and central causes positional causes
Secondary aims
 determine the prevalence of peripheral and central causes of positional nystagmus in
the North Central London area
 identify the number of treatments required for each canal type of BPPV within the cohort
and through an International modified Delphi Consensus
 establish the recurrence rates for different types of BPPV
 explore if there is an anatomical difference to the inner ear for those with non resolving
BPPV
This study aims to expand the limited literature to help clinicians identify and distinguish
between peripheral and central positional nystagmus more effectively. This will improve access
to the correct treatment in a more timely manner, and enhance patient care and experience.
The study will also fill a knowledge gap in understanding how many treatments are required for
each sub type of BPPV. Prior to this application, I conducted a preliminary survey to explore the
overall management of BPPV across a group of 24 multidisciplinary professionals, attending a
specialist conference for dizziness in the UK. The results of this have informed the research
protocol, but a larger scale Delphi consensus opinion will be sought as part of this proposal to
inform best practice of BPPV, worldwide.
Finally, this study will include a novel case control exploration as to whether there is an
anatomical explanation for why BPPV does not respond to the gold standard manaourvres. This
will be the first study, to the applicant's knowledge, using 3D reconstructive magnetic
resonance imaging in peripheral positional nystagmus.
Project Plan
Observational study
Recruitment
Adults (over 16 years old) reporting dizziness, vertigo, or unsteadiness that is triggered by head
or body movement will be eligible to participate. Exclusion criteria include: medical instability
(cardiovascular, cervical, or neurological), spontaneous nystagmus in sitting, pregnancy,
previous history of existing and persistent vertigo, and lacking capacity to consent. Those
unable to understand English will also be excluded, as the questionnaires used in this study and
in the vestibular specialism, have not been validated and translated into every language.
This prospective study will be open for recruitment for 18 months. A purposive sample will be
recruited. Figure 1 summarises the flow of each participant through the study duration.

Figure 1: Participant flow through the study.

Power Calculations
Current research lacks detailed prevalence data on positional nystagmus across healthcare
settings, failing to differentiate between peripheral and central causes. The most relevant data
from a French neuro-otology clinic over nine months indicates that 20% of patients with
symptoms of vertigo and unsteadiness showed positional nystagmus, with breakdowns into
BPPV (80%), other peripheral causes (3%), central causes (12%), and undetermined (5%)
(Bertholon et al., 2006).
To ensure sufficient statistical power for detecting the 20% prevalence rate of positional
nystagmus identified in previous studies (Bertholon et al., 2006), we calculated the necessary
sample size using the formula n= [(z)2 (p)(1-p)]/(d)2 where Z is 1.96 for 95% confidence, p is 0.20,
and d is 0.05 (Sharma et al., 2019; Pourhoseingholi et al., 2013). This calculation resulted in a
sample size of approximately 246 adults, which is sufficient to provide reliable data while
ensuring the study remains scalable and manageable within the constraints of our resources.
Participant recruitment
The study will be conducted at a single site, based at The National Hospital for Neurology and
Neurosurgery. This will ensure access to specialist equipment (Balcare chair) for
standardisation of testing, to address the study aims and as recommended by researchers
(Lemos). The study will be embedded within the Acute vertigo service, and Same Day
Emergency Care service (SDEC), at University College London Hospitals (UCLH) with oversight
by Consultant Neurologists. This would also be the location patients from the North Central
London area would attend for routine clinical care, and hence this reduces any additional travel
burden or cost.
The North Central London (NCL) area is home to a diverse range of ethnicities, that includes
white, Asian, and Black people. The largest ethnic minority communities in this area of London
are Turkish, Irish, Polish and Asian. Discussions with patients from the NCL area, revealed they
wanted to be involved in this study, but raised concerns about covering the travel costs. One
person (Mr M) also raised concerns about other people he knew being able to fully understand
and speak English well enough to take part. Therefore, funding will be requested to cover travel
expenses and also cover costs of any interpreters that are required.
The study will be advertised through local GP network and community services for North
Central London. This will include approximately 200 GP surgeries, and community neurology
teams in Barnet, Camden, Enfield, Haringey, and Islington. It will also be advertised internally at
UCLH through email communication and attendance at departmental meetings. This broad
recruitment strategy will ensure the desired sample size will be met in the allocated recruitment
time.
Advertisements will include screening questions for positional dizziness and unsteadiness.
These questions have been adopted from a diagnostic framework for vestibular causes of
dizziness (Male et al), which had PPI involvement and a modified Delphi consensus.
If potential participants answer the questions as indicated on the advertisement, the participant
or clinician will be encouraged to contact the research team to make a referral. Patients helped
to determine the best recruitment approach, and reported they would like an email and a
telephone option. These contact details of the research team will be included on the
advertisements.

Participants will be able to self-refer, or be referred by their GP or community therapy services
from within North Central London areas. Internal referrals from UCLH emergency department
(ED), falls service, the hyper-acute stroke unit (HASU), and from therapy services will also be
accepted.
Potential participants will be contacted by a member of the research team and provided with
the participant information sheet by email. They will be given the opportunity to ask questions,
prior to providing consent. Consent will be provided electronically through a central REDCap
database set up for this project. If potential participants request the consent form in paper
format, the signed hard copy will be loaded in to the secure database.
They will then be invited to attend the hospital for assessment and treatment as the earliest
point in time. The GP will be informed of their participation in the study.
Data Management
Potential participants' data will be identifiable for the purpose of follow up, and potential 3D
scanning. However, it will be anonymised for any dissemination. All data will be kept
confidential and secure by the research team and stored electronically in a central REDCap
database. The REDCap database will require a password to access which will only be known by
the research team.
Eye movement recordings will be stored on the Balcare laptop and secure drive. This will
require a password to access, and the data will be handled securely in line with UCLH routine
data governance regulations.
Research protocol
Participants will electronically complete routine questionnaires used in dizziness and balance
services (see Table 1) and provide more personal information relating to their health. This will
help identify any accompanying symptoms for peripheral and central causes of positional
nystagmus.
Patient reported questionnaire Details
Dizziness handicap inventory (DHI) A measure of self-perceived disability
attributable to vestibular disease.
Subjects score 25 questions which are
classified into three domain levels; Yes = 4,
Sometimes = 2, No = 0 with a total
percentage score calculated.
Scores >20 indicate dizziness/unsteadiness
is having a significant impact on their life
(Jacobson and Newman, 1990).
Situational Vertigo Questionnaire (SVQ) Evaluates the extent that certain situations
cause symptoms of vertigo or makes patients
feel worse.
Each activity rated between 0 (not at all) and
4 (very much) with a not tried (NT) option
available.
Scores >1 indicate visual induced dizziness
and/or imbalance (Guerraz et al., 2001).
State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI – form Y) A self-reported assessment of trait and state
anxiety.

Subjects rate 20 statements using a 4-point
Likert scale between 1 (not at all) to 4 (very
much so).
Higher scores indicate greater anxiety (Julian,
2011).
Visually Induced Motion Sickness A self reported tool to estimate the
Susceptibility Questionnaire (VIMSSQ - short) susceptibility to motion sickness when using
devices and watching moving visual content.
Subjects rate six devices for how often they
experience nausea, dizziness, headache,
fatigue, and eye strain using a 4-point Likert
scale.
Higher scores indicate greater motion
sickness susceptibility (Golding et al 2021)
Table 1: Details of the questionnaires each participant will complete as part of the initial visit.
Participants will then be assessed, by a specialist physiotherapist, using a purpose-built chair
(Balcare). This chair allows accurate assessment, irrespective of the patient's physical mobility
level or co-morbidities, and without any increased manual handling demands for the clinician. It
provides a standardised approach to assessment and uses video goggles that helps clinicians
to interpret different types of nystagmus. This will allow more accurate interpretation and
quantification of peripheral positional nystagmus from central positional nystagmus.
Each assessment visit will take no more than one hour. It will include a lying and standing blood
pressure check for any postural hypotension, and a full eye movement examination in sitting
using the video goggles (fixation removed). The eye movement examination includes observing
for spontaneous nystagmus, gaze evoked nystagmus, and abnormalities in smooth pursuit and
saccades. Positional tests using the Balcare chair will then be completed, whilst wearing the
video goggles. These will include Dix Hallpike on both sides, Roll Tests, straight head hanging
and sitting back up.
Should BPPV be identified as per the diagnostic criteria (ref), then the appropriate treatment
manoeuvre will be provided once using the Balcare chair. This may include the modified Epley
manoeuvre, Semont liberatory manoeuvre, Gufoni manoeuvre (geotropic or apogeotropic),
Lempert BBQ roll, or the Yacovino manoeuvre (Refs?). Each of these manoeuvres take no more
than 3 minutes to complete using the Balcare chair.
These patients will be followed up at the next convenient time (within four weeks) and re-treated
once per visit if required, in order to make sure the problem has fully resolved. They will be
invited to re-attend for subsequent re-assessment and treatment, at four weekly intervals up to
a maximum of three treatments, as informed by the preparatory survey of specialists. In this
time, if the assessment manoeuvres are negative, this will end the participants involvement in
the study.
Any patients that do not respond to three treatments, will then be referred to the acute vertigo
service for review by a Consultant Neurologist, as per the consensus of specialists from the
preparatory survey. They will also be invited to have high resolution 3D reconstructive magnetic
resonance imaging of the inner ear. The imaging will be compared to a purposive group of BPPV
patients (n=12) whose symptoms resolved within three manoeuvres to detect any anatomical
difference that may explain the lack of resolution. Differences in the angle, orientation, width,
and the viscosity of the endolymph will be compared to responders, using computational

methods. This may then allow adjustments to be made to the treatment sequence to optimally
treat and resolve the BPPV.
Participants that do not have the nystagmus characteristics expected for BPPV within the
positional tests, or present with abnormalities in the oculomotor examination indicating central
problems, will be referred to the acute vertigo service for further assessment and management
by a Neurologist. This may include medication or further imaging. This will end the participants
involvement in the study.
Any participant will be invited to contact the research team, at any point during the recruitment
phase of the study, should they feel their symptoms have recurred. They will then follow the
same intervention pathway as new participants (see Figure 1).
Statistical analysis
Descriptive and predictive statistical analyses will be performed. Categorical data will be
presented as frequencies with percentages. Continuous data will be presented as means and
Standard Deviations (±SD) where data is normally distributed. Medians and Inter-Quartile
Ranges (IQR) will be used when the data is not normally distributed. Appropriate regression
models will be used to investigate the association between individual clinical features and
diagnoses of peripheral or central causes of positional nystagmus/dizziness.
3D reconstructive MRI scans of participants not responding to BPPV manoeuvres, will be
compared to those that do respond to explore if there is an anatomical difference. How?
Delphi expert opinion
In addition to this observational study, a modified Delphi technique will be used to gain
consensus from a multi disciplinary international group of clinicians working with vestibular
conditions (Trevelyan and Robinson, 2015). This will include Consultants with a primary interest
and with >5 years experience in clinical neuro-otology or audiovestibular medicine, ENT
Consultants, Neurology Consultants or GPs working with dizziness. In addition, physical
therapists, and audiologists with >5 year's experience of working with vestibular conditions will
also be included. There will be three rounds to the Delphi process.
Participants will be identified through the membership lists of the European Academy of
Neurology (EAN) specialist panel of neuro-otologists, Barany Society, the vestibular
rehabilitation special interest groups for the American Physical Therapy Association (APTA), the
Association of Chartered Physiotherapists Interested in Vestibular Rehabiliation (ACPIVR), and
the British Society of Audiology (BSA).
Round one
This will be a survey to explore participants management of the different subtypes of BPPV,
expanding the preparatory survey results. The responses will be anonymous and confidential.
Membership secretaries will be sent an email by a member of the research team, with specific
wording to forward on to members asking them to participate. Within the email invitation, will be
a link for the free survey using Google forms.
The membership secretaries will send a reminder email one week later, in order to maximise
participation.

Round two
Round two will be informed by round one. Statements will be developed from the survey
answers with the highest scores, and these will be emailed via the membership secretaries of
the above mentioned international groups.
Respondents will be asked to rate how much they agree or disagree with the statements using a
six-point scale. Choosing a scale without a midpoint will avoid neutral responses and will make
respondents make a positive or negative decision about each question (Trevelyan and
Robinson, 2015). They will also be asked to add any statements they feel should be included or
suggest any that need rewording.
The membership secretaries will send a reminder email one week later. Consensus per
statement will be defined when >75% of the responses are in the lower or higher tertile
(Boulkedid et al., 2011).
The responses will be anonymous and confidential. However, at this stage, respondents will be
asked if they would like to be part of a panel discussion in order to discuss any points that have
not reached consensus. If respondents are interested, they will be asked to provide an email
address and name so they can be contacted.
Round three
This will involve a panel discussion of volunteers from round two. A time will be established that
will accommodate as many of those interested as possible, accounting for time differences and
the individuals' commitments.
The panel discussion will attempt to reach a majority consensus on any remaining statements.
Any points that do not reach majority consensus, will have recommendations made for future
research.
Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
The results from this project will be submitted to relevant peer reviewed journals for publication.
I anticipate at least three papers that will include describing the nystagmus features of the
largest cohort of patients with positional nystagmus and any key distinguishing features, sharing
the outcome of the International and multidisciplinary Delphi consensus, and a paper to share
the findings from the first study to date undertaking 3D reconstructive imaging of the inner ear.
I will also submit these results to specialist conferences, to present as either a poster or
platform presentation. This includes the Barany Society, and the British Society of Neuro-
Otology. These conferences are attended by specialist doctors, physiotherapists, audiologists,
and other members of the multidisciplinary team working with dizziness and balance patients,
and are internationally renowned. ? ED/other medical ones ?
As a member of the physiotherapy interest group (ACPIVR), I will also share my results at either
annual national or regional training events, and through the quarterly journal. I will embed the
findings in any education I continue to provide through national and international courses, and
through university masters programs.
I will also share the results through a range of social media platforms, including the research
unit at UCL, ACPIVR, my own personal accounts, and the Ménière's Disease society. I will also

share the findings as a newsletter to the service users that assisted in recruitment, both in the
community and at UCLH. I will also offer to present the findings at any local departmental
meetings.
As part of this project, I plan to set up a PPI advisory group and will seek their help on how best
to disseminate the findings so results are shared with the people it affects, and improves care in
the future.
Project management
The project has been discussed with the NIHR Research Support Service (RSS) and xxxxx at the
UCL/UCLH Joint Research Office. The project has been developed with support from a
specialist Neurologist that leads the SENSE research unit in the Institute of Neurology and a
specialist Neuroradiologist.
Costings have been approved by the xxxxx based in xxxxx.
Ethics
Once funding is approved, I will apply for internal ethical approval through the Joint Research
Office at UCLH. I will also complete the Integrated Research Application System (IRAS) form
and apply for NHS ethical approval, as this study will include patients.
Success criteria
